one_line_takeaway: ABJ shows that safety alignment focused on input filtering is insufficient: by transforming a harmful request into neutral text/image data and letting the target model's own chain-of-thought reasoning reconstruct the harmful content, it achieves ~80-88% ASR on aligned LLMs/VLMs and defeats input-stage guardrails, exposing the reasoning process itself as an underdefended attack surface.
key_finding: ABJ achieves high attack success while defeating input-stage defenses that prior obfuscation-based attacks do not evade as cleanly. On LLMs it reaches 82.1% ASR on GPT-4o-2024-11-20, 80.7% on Claude-3-haiku, 85.1% on Llama-3-8B-Instruct, 84.7% on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, and 88.2% on DeepSeek-V3 (ASR-E up to 92.1%), versus a best baseline (DRA) of only 41.1% on GPT-4o. Reasoning models are more resilient but still vulnerable: 40.2% ASR on o1-2024-12-17 and 58.7% on DeepSeek-R1. VLMs are as vulnerable as LLMs via the visual path (85.5% GPT-4o, 88.6% InternVL3-8B, 89.7% Qwen2.5-VL). Five input-stage safeguards barely dent ABJ (average ASR reduction: 0% Moderation, 0.9% PPL, 4.0% Paraphrase, 5.7% RA-LLM, 6.3% Llama Guard). The most effective defenses target reasoning directly: a reasoning-verification system prompt cuts average ASR below 30%, and SFT+DPO on Llama-3-8B lowers ASR from 85.1% to 60.8% (SFT alone to 77.4%) but does not eliminate the vulnerability.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Analyzing-based Jailbreak (ABJ), a black-box jailbreak attack that shifts the attack surface from the conventional input-level manipulation paradigm to the model's own multi-step reasoning process. Rather than smuggling a harmful query through obfuscated input (the approach of prior work the authors position ABJ against), ABJ removes the original harmful query entirely (an 'implicit attack' where S_implicit(X) does not contain X) and instead supplies semantically neutral data that steers the target model to infer harmful content during chain-of-thought reasoning. The authors' own novel elements are: (1) framing reasoning-level manipulation as an underexplored threat vector; (2) a two-stage automated pipeline (Attack Initiation that transforms a harmful query into neutral textual attributes and a corresponding image, then Attack Execution that drives the model through reasoning along independent textual and visual paths); (3) a toxicity-adjustment mechanism that iteratively raises or lowers the toxicity of randomly selected attributes based on whether the model refuses or answers benignly; and (4) a unified multimodal framework covering LLMs, VLMs, and reasoning models (RLMs).
threat_model: Black-box attacker with only query access to a safety-aligned target model (no weights, gradients, or logits required; white-box baselines like GCG are included only for comparison). The attacker's goal is to elicit harmful content on prohibited tasks (e.g., AdvBench harmful behaviors) while evading both internal alignment and external input-stage guardrails. The attacker controls the full prompt and can iterate up to a maximum number of steps, and uses auxiliary assistant models (GLM-4-9B-0414 for text transformation/toxicity adjustment, gpt-image-1 for image synthesis) that are separate from the target. Target system prompts are removed in the evaluation. The core exploited weakness is that most non-reasoning models (and, to a lesser degree, RLMs) lack the ability to verify or reflect on the safety of their own intermediate reasoning chain, so harmful intent that emerges only during reasoning bypasses input-stage detection.
limitations: Author-stated: ABJ's success depends heavily on the target model's multi-step reasoning ability and degrades on smaller/weaker models; it is evaluated only in single-turn attacks on individual models, leaving multi-agent and multi-turn settings unexplored; and only common defenses (system prompts, external guardrails, SFT/DPO) are assessed, not more advanced training-based defenses that constrain the reasoning process. Reviewer-added observations: the relatively lower ASR on RLMs (40.2% on o1) shows reasoning-reflection capability materially reduces effectiveness, so the attack's premise weakens as models gain self-verification; the pipeline depends on external assistant models (GLM-4-9B, gpt-image-1) whose behavior affects reproducibility; evaluation is confined to the single AdvBench dataset; and while judgment used a validated prompt-based evaluator plus manual review, ASR is inherently sensitive to the judge definition of 'unsafe'.

datasets_used: AdvBench Harmful Behaviors (520 task instructions) used as the attack evaluation set

Custom validation dataset of 400 hand-labeled samples (evenly split across Safe Refusal, Harmful Content, Constructive Instruction, and Harmful Content with Constructive Instruction) used to select the harmfulness judge
3,000 ABJ and random-ABJ samples generated from experiments, used to SFT/DPO fine-tune Llama-3-8B-Instruct for the defense study
quantitative_results: ABJ ASR on LLMs: 82.1% GPT-4o-2024-11-20, 80.7% Claude-3-haiku-20240307, 85.1% Llama-3-8B-Instruct, 84.7% Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, 88.2% DeepSeek-V3 (Table 1)
ABJ ASR-Ensemble on LLMs up to 92.1% (DeepSeek-V3); 84.2% GPT-4o, 83.2% Claude-3-haiku, 88.6% Llama-3-8B, 89.3% Qwen2.5-7B (Table 1)
ABJ ASR on RLMs: 40.2% o1-2024-12-17, 58.7% DeepSeek-R1 (Table 2)
ABJ ASR on VLMs (visual path): 85.5% GPT-4o-2024-11-20, 88.6% InternVL3-8B-Instruct, 89.7% Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct (Table 2)
Best baseline on GPT-4o was DRA at 41.1% ASR, roughly half of ABJ's 82.1% (Table 1)
Average ASR reduction under input-stage safeguards: Moderation 0%, PPL 0.9%, Paraphrase 4.0%, RA-LLM 5.7%, Llama Guard 6.3% (Table 3)
Random ABJ average ASR: 81.5% textual (-2.7%) and 80.6% visual (-7.3%) vs standard ABJ 84.2% textual / 87.9% visual (Table 4)
Defenses: reasoning-verification system prompt lowers average ASR below 30%; SFT lowers Llama-3-8B ASR from 85.1% to 77.4%, and adding DPO further to 60.8%
